title: Suffering and Glory
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 6-2-24 -AM- Suffering and Glory - 1 Peter 4_12-19 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Sermon 30 on 1 Peter — Two Themes: Suffering and Glory
subpoints: Peter in 1 Peter 4:12-19 continues the theme begun in 2:11-12: living as sojourners in a hostile world
Suffering and glory are inescapably connected in Peter's mind — suffering is bearable because it points to glory, and glory carries us through suffering
Central question: How do suffering and glory work together?

outline.title: What Kind of Suffering Is Peter Addressing?
subpoints: Peter addresses one specific kind of suffering: suffering as a consequence of faithful testimony to the Lord Jesus Christ
'Christian' appears only three times in the Bible: Acts 11:26 (first called Christians in Antioch), Acts 26 (Agrippa's 'almost' response), and 1 Peter 4:16
The word 'Christian' likely means 'follower of Christ' — originally used as a term of derision
This suffering is suffering for righteousness' sake (1 Peter 3:14), for doing what is right (1 Peter 3:17), not for being a jerk or sinning
Peter writes to five provinces in Asia Minor — different levels of persecution in different regions; some preparing, some already enduring

outline.title: Peter's Two Goals: Preparation and Comfort
subpoints: Peter has a goal of preparation (for those not yet persecuted) and a goal of comfort (for those currently suffering)
There are only two categories of Christians: those who will suffer and those who are suffering for the sake of Christ
Current Western church context: we are in the 'negative world' — Aaron Renn's framework of positive world (pre-1994), neutral world (1994-2014), negative world (2014-present)
In the negative world, Christian moral views are seen as a public threat, and openly subscribing to them brings consequences
The US church has never faced real persecution, which may explain prosperity theology, worldly worship, and accommodation of unbiblical gender ideology
Judgment begins at the house of God (1 Peter 4:17) — God purifies His church through suffering; this is His mercy

outline.title: The Theme of Glory in 1 Peter 4
subpoints: The word 'glory' is used 11 times in 1 Peter
Glory in 4:11 ('that in all things God may be glorified'); 4:13 ('when His glory is revealed'); 4:14 ('the spirit of glory and of God rests upon you')
The glory of man fades (1 Peter 1:24); the glory of God is eternal and belongs to Jesus forever
The spirit of glory resting on the suffering believer (4:14) is a present reality
Only the perspective of the glory to come makes this life bearable and even enjoyable — without it we are of all men most pitiable
Heaven, the presence of God, the glory of God — these must motivate us to keep going

outline.title: The Cost of Missions and the Call to Suffer for Christ
subpoints: The vast majority of the world's population lives in places where believing in Jesus will make life miserable
At least 69 countries restrict or are hostile to Christianity; 13 forbid Bible ownership; in North Korea, owning a Bible is punishable by death
The most likely way the unreached will hear about Jesus is if people like us go and tell them
The cost of bringing Jesus to the unreached can be our lives or the lives of our children — and that cannot stop us

Paul's mindset: 'What things were gain to me, these I have counted loss for Christ. Yet indeed I also count all things loss for the excellence of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord' (Philippians 3:7-8)
When the church thinks salvation is more important than comfort, the nations will be glad (Psalm 96-97)

outline.title: How Suffering and Glory Work Together
subpoints: Answer: The glory of the life to come is so massive and attractive that the sufferings of this life that come from obeying God are inconsequential by comparison
2 Corinthians 4:16-18: 'Our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all'
When we suffer well for Christ's sake, the temporary glories of this world grow dim as the glory of heaven shines brighter

Charles Hodge: 'We would in a single word state what it is we desire — it is that the students should be made to feel that the eternal is infinitely more important than the temporal, the heavenly than the earthly'

summary: This is the 30th sermon in the 1 Peter series, preached on the occasion of the baptism of a covenant child (Olivia). The sermon addresses 1 Peter 4:12-19 and the inseparable connection between suffering and glory in Peter's theology. Pastor Lyro identifies the specific kind of suffering Peter addresses — suffering as a consequence of faithful testimony to Christ — noting that the word 'Christian' appears only three times in the Bible and was originally a term of derision. He situates the contemporary church in Aaron Renn's framework of the 'negative world' (post-2014), in which Christian moral views are seen as a public threat, and suggests that the American church's lack of persecution may have contributed to its theological drift. He then unpacks the pervasive theme of glory in 1 Peter 4 — the spirit of glory resting on the suffering believer, the glory awaiting at Christ's revelation — and issues a mission call: the most likely way the unreached will hear about Jesus is if we go to them, at whatever cost. The sermon's answer to its central question is that the immensity of future glory makes present suffering for Christ bearable and even joyful. In connection with the baptism, the latter portion of the sermon is a full treatment of the biblical and theological basis for covenant (infant) baptism, grounding it in the continuity of the covenant sign from circumcision to baptism (Colossians 2:11-12; Galatians 3:26-29) and emphasizing the solemn parental responsibility that accompanies it.
transcript:
## Introduction: Suffering and Glory in 1 Peter

This is our 30th sermon on 1 Peter — for such a short book, that is a lot of sermons. And the more I read through 1 Peter, the more the richness of this short epistle comes to the fore.

Peter in this passage continues the discussion he began in chapter 2, verses 11 and 12, about living a Christian life in a hostile world. A Christian life is in opposition to a worldly life, and the world knows that. Since chapter 2, Peter has been developing that, encouraging us to live as sojourners, as pilgrims, and that our way of life, our conduct, may be honorable to God among the Gentiles, so that even when we are persecuted for our faith, God is glorified in His judging of those who persecute His people. Peter tells us, as a theme of the epistle: entrust your soul to God and keep on doing good — He will vindicate us.

What I want us to do today is consider two themes that are all over the book and at the core of this passage: the theme of suffering and glory. Suffering and glory are inescapably connected in Peter's mind. They exist together and they exist for the sake of one another. Suffering is bearable because it points to glory, and glory carries us through suffering. The question we will attempt to answer at the very end is this: how do glory and suffering work together?

## What Kind of Suffering Is Peter Addressing?

We have to ask ourselves first: what is the suffering that Peter is so passionate about in this letter? Peter is addressing one specific kind of suffering: suffering that is a consequence of having a faithful testimony to the Lord Jesus Christ. Suffering that comes from being a Christian. In chapter 4 verse 14, he says: when you are reproached or insulted for the name of Christ, then count that glory. And in verse 16: "If anyone suffers as a Christian, let him not be ashamed, but let him glorify God in this matter."

This is one of only three times the word "Christian" is used in the Bible. The first is in Acts 11:26, where the followers of Christ were first called Christians in Antioch. The second is in Acts 26, in the lips of Agrippa, who tells Paul, "You almost persuade me to become a Christian" — some of the saddest words in the Bible. And the word "Christian" just means follower of Christ. When you see how prevalent the word is today in the Western world, you would think it was all over the Bible — yet those who believe in Christ are more often called believers, disciples, saints. And it seems like at least in the Acts references, the word was originally used as a pejorative, a term of derision. What glory, to be accused of being a follower of Christ and to suffer for it.

This kind of suffering comes from obeying what God says — suffering for righteousness' sake (3:14). Peter makes that clear: it is not the suffering that comes from being a jerk, not the suffering that comes from our own sin, but the suffering for following the Lord Jesus Christ. And it is becoming the norm as our culture becomes more and more secularized.

Social commentator Aaron Renn suggests we are now in the period called the negative world. Before 1994, we lived in a positive world: society held a mostly positive view of Christianity; being a good church-going man was part of being a respectable citizen; Christian moral norms were the basic moral norms of society. Then from 1994 to 2014, the neutral world: Christianity was no longer privileged but not disfavored; being a Christian was one valid option among many. And then from 2014 to the present, the negative world: society has come to have a negative view of Christianity; being known as a Christian is a social negative in elite domains; Christian morality is expressly repudiated as a threat to the public good; subscribing to Christian moral views brings negative consequences. We saw this recently in Indiana, where a Christian couple had their children taken away because they would not support their child's transgender desires.

## Peter's Two Goals: Preparation and Comfort

Peter has two goals: preparation for those about to face suffering, and comfort for those already in it. There are only two categories of Christians: those who will suffer and those who are suffering for the sake of Christ. Peter is writing to churches in a four-province region with different levels of persecution — some already active, some preparing. He mixes preparation and comfort throughout the book.

I think we in the United States are in the being-prepared-for-persecution state. We are not generally facing active persecution for the sake of the gospel, though it has been encroaching. And perhaps this is one reason there are so many issues within the American church: prosperity theology is well accepted; the life we live now seems more important than the life to come; acceptance of unbiblical gender ideology is becoming more common in so-called evangelical churches; worship services are shaped by a desire to please men instead of God. I wonder if we have experienced this because we have not faced persecution for righteousness' sake.

And brothers and sisters, we need to be prepared for God's work of purification in His church, because He is too merciful to allow His church to remain unfaithful to Him. Judgment begins at the house of God (1 Peter 4:17) — God purifies His church through the suffering of His people. He does this as a loving Father, as Hebrews 12 tells us.

Peter says in 1 Peter 1:6-9: "In this you greatly rejoice, though now for a little while, if need be, you have been grieved by various trials — if need be," as if there is a need for trials in the church — "that the genuineness of your faith, being much more precious than gold that perishes, though it is tested by fire, may be found to praise, honor, and glory at the revelation of Jesus Christ."

## The Theme of Glory

Suffering for the sake of the gospel comes with glory. Look at just the glory talk around our passage in 1 Peter 4. Verse 11: "That in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ." Verse 11 again: "To whom belong the glory and the dominion forever and ever." Verse 13: "That when His glory is revealed, you may also be glad with exceeding joy." Verse 14: "Blessed are you, for the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you."

Glory is used 11 times in this little book. Glory comes from the idea of having a high opinion of someone because he is worthy of it. In the case of God, His glory is the excellence of His being, of who He is. The glory of man fades away like the grass withers — it is June 2nd and some of my grass is already brown — that is the idea of the glory of us without Christ. But the glory of God is eternal and unchangeable and lasts forever. There is no dry season in that.

If we do not have that perspective — if heaven means nothing to us — we are of all men most pitiable. There is no point to this life except to eat, drink, and be merry, for tomorrow we die. And yet there is glory. The only way that you and I will be willing to suffer for the sake of the gospel in this life is if the glory of the life to come is something we truly look forward to.

## The Call to Missions

Let Peter's comfort and encouragement to Christians who are suffering because of their faith be also a call to us. The vast majority of the world's population lives in places where believing in Jesus will make their lives miserable. Most of them do not know who Jesus is. The most likely way they will hear anything about Jesus is if people like you and me go and tell them.

Brothers and sisters, we need to count the cost. The cost of bringing Jesus to people who have never believed can very well be our lives or the lives of our children. And that cannot stop us. The cost is the suffering and the loss of all things for the sake of Christ and for the sake of the souls of countless men, women, and children. That's the cost. And the Lord Jesus Christ was willing to pay that cost for you and for me. He died; He suffered unjustly because of you and because of me. But the reward is sharing in the glory of God.

"What things were gain to me, these I have counted loss for Christ. Yet indeed I also count all things loss for the excellence of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord" — that was Paul's mindset as the representative Christian. When we get to the place where the souls of people are more important to us than our own well-being, then the nations will be glad. Psalm 96:3: "Declare His glory among the nations, His wonders among all peoples."

## How Suffering and Glory Work Together

So remember the question we began with: how do glory and suffering work together? The glory of the life to come is so massive and attractive to us that the sufferings of this life that come from obeying the Word of God are inconsequential.

2 Corinthians 4:16-18: "Therefore we do not lose heart. Even though our outward man is perishing, yet the inward man is being renewed day by day. For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory, while we do not look at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen. For the things which are seen are temporary, but the things which are not seen are eternal."

When we suffer well for Christ's sake, the temporary glories of this world grow dim as the glory of heaven shines brighter before us. Charles Hodge, in a speech welcoming the new president of Princeton College, said: "We would in a single word state what it is that we desire — it is that the students should be made to feel that the eternal is infinitely more important than the temporal, the heavenly than the earthly." That is what we desire for us as well.

Father, thank you that you are a God who speaks and through your Word directs us to you. We thank you for the encouragement, the preparation, and the comfort for suffering for righteousness' sake. Give us grace to stand in suffering, if you have it for us, for the sake of Christ, and help us to do so by looking at your glory — the glory of heaven and the life to come. In Jesus' name. Amen.
